title: Damage and Healing Systems
description: Comprehensive rules for damage, healing, hit points, and character survival

hit_points.description: Hit Points represent durability and the will to live. Creatures with more Hit Points are more difficult to kill
hit_points.maximum: Your Hit Point maximum is the number of Hit Points you have when uninjured
hit_points.current_range: Your current Hit Points can be any number from that maximum down to 0, which is the lowest Hit Points can go
hit_points.damage_effect: Whenever you take damage, subtract it from your Hit Points. Hit Point loss has no effect on your capabilities until you reach 0 Hit Points
hit_points.bloodied: If you have half your Hit Points or fewer, you're Bloodied, which has no game effect on its own but which might trigger other game effects

damage_rolls.description: Each weapon, spell, and damaging monster ability specifies the damage it deals. You roll the damage dice, add any modifiers, and deal the damage to your target
damage_rolls.minimum_damage: If there's a penalty to the damage, it's possible to deal 0 damage but not negative damage
damage_rolls.weapon_damage: When attacking with a weapon, you add your ability modifier—the same modifier used for the attack roll—to the damage roll
damage_rolls.spell_damage: A spell tells you which dice to roll for damage and whether to add any modifiers
damage_rolls.fixed_damage: Unless a rule says otherwise, you don't add your ability modifier to a fixed damage amount that doesn't use a roll, such as the damage of a Blowgun

critical_hits.description: When you score a Critical Hit, you deal extra damage
critical_hits.mechanic: Roll the attack's damage dice twice, add them together, and add any relevant modifiers as normal
critical_hits.example: For example, if you score a Critical Hit with a Dagger, roll 2d4 for the damage rather than 1d4, and add your relevant ability modifier
critical_hits.additional_dice: If the attack involves other damage dice, such as from the Rogue's Sneak Attack feature, you also roll those dice twice

multiple_targets.description: When you create a damaging effect that forces two or more targets to make saving throws against it at the same time, roll the damage once for all the targets
multiple_targets.example: For example, when a wizard casts Fireball, the spell's damage is rolled once for all creatures caught in the blast

half_damage.description: Many saving throw effects deal half damage (round down) to a target when the target succeeds on the saving throw
half_damage.calculation: The halved damage is equal to half the damage that would be dealt on a failed save

damage_types.description: Each instance of damage has a type, like Fire or Slashing. Damage types are listed in the Rules Glossary and have no rules of their own, but other rules, such as Resistance, rely on damage types

resistance.description: If you have Resistance to a damage type, damage of that type is halved against you (round down)
resistance.example: If you have Resistance to Cold damage, such damage is halved against you

vulnerability.description: If you have Vulnerability to a damage type, damage of that type is doubled against you
vulnerability.example: If you have Vulnerability to Fire damage, such damage is doubled against you

resistance_vulnerability.no_stacking: Multiple instances of Resistance or Vulnerability that affect the same damage type count as only one instance. For example, if you have Resistance to Necrotic damage as well as Resistance to all damage, Necrotic damage is reduced by half against you

order_of_application.description: Modifiers to damage are applied in the following order: adjustments such as bonuses, penalties, or multipliers are applied first; Resistance is applied second; and Vulnerability is applied third
order_of_application.example: A creature has Resistance to all damage and Vulnerability to Fire damage, and it's within a magical aura that reduces all damage by 5. If it takes 28 Fire damage, the damage is first reduced by 5 (to 23), then halved for the creature's Resistance (and rounded down to 11), then doubled for its Vulnerability (to 22)

immunity.damage_immunity: Immunity to a damage type means you don't take damage of that type
immunity.condition_immunity: Immunity to a condition means you aren't affected by it

healing.sources: Hit Points can be restored by magic, such as the Cure Wounds spell or a Potion of Healing, or by a Short or Long Rest
healing.application: When you receive healing, add the restored Hit Points to your current Hit Points
healing.maximum_limit: Your Hit Points can't exceed your Hit Point maximum, so any Hit Points regained in excess of the maximum are lost
healing.example: For example, if you receive 8 Hit Points of healing and have 14 Hit Points and a Hit Point maximum of 20, you regain 6 Hit Points, not 8

dropping_to_zero.description: When a creature drops to 0 Hit Points, it either dies outright or falls unconscious

instant_death.monster_death: A monster dies the instant it drops to 0 Hit Points, although a Dungeon Master can ignore this rule for an individual monster and treat it like a character
instant_death.hit_point_maximum_zero: A creature dies if its Hit Point maximum reaches 0. Certain effects drain life energy, reducing a creature's Hit Point maximum
instant_death.massive_damage: When damage reduces a character to 0 Hit Points and damage remains, the character dies if the remainder equals or exceeds their Hit Point maximum. For example, if your character has a Hit Point maximum of 12, currently has 6 Hit Points, and takes 18 damage, the character drops to 0 Hit Points, but 12 damage remains. The character then dies, since 12 equals their Hit Point maximum

dropping_to_zero.falling_unconscious: If you reach 0 Hit Points and don't die instantly, you have the Unconscious condition until you regain any Hit Points, and you now face making Death Saving Throws

death_saving_throws.description: Whenever you start your turn with 0 Hit Points, you must make a Death Saving Throw to determine whether you creep closer to death or hang on to life. Unlike other saving throws, this one isn't tied to an ability score. You're in the hands of fate now

mechanic.roll: Roll 1d20. If the roll is 10 or higher, you succeed. Otherwise, you fail
mechanic.tracking: A success or failure has no effect by itself. On your third success, you become Stable. On your third failure, you die
mechanic.non_consecutive: The successes and failures don't need to be consecutive; keep track of both until you collect three of a kind. The number of both is reset to zero when you regain any Hit Points or become Stable

special_rolls.natural_1: When you roll a 1 on the d20 for a Death Saving Throw, you suffer two failures
special_rolls.natural_20: If you roll a 20 on the d20, you regain 1 Hit Point

death_saving_throws.damage_at_zero: If you take any damage while you have 0 Hit Points, you suffer a Death Saving Throw failure. If the damage is from a Critical Hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum, you die

stabilizing.help_action: You can take the Help action to try to stabilize a creature with 0 Hit Points, which requires a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check
stabilizing.stable_condition: A Stable creature doesn't make Death Saving Throws even though it has 0 Hit Points, but it still has the Unconscious condition
stabilizing.taking_damage: If the creature takes damage, it stops being Stable and starts making Death Saving Throws again
stabilizing.natural_recovery: A Stable creature that isn't healed regains 1 Hit Point after 1d4 hours

knockout_rule.description: When you would reduce a creature to 0 Hit Points with a melee attack, you can instead reduce the creature to 1 Hit Point and give it the Unconscious condition
knockout_rule.recovery: It then starts a Short Rest, at the end of which that condition ends on it
knockout_rule.early_end: The condition ends early if the creature regains any Hit Points or if someone takes an action to administer first aid to it, making a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check

temporary_hit_points.description: Some spells and other effects confer Temporary Hit Points, which are a buffer against losing actual Hit Points
temporary_hit_points.precedence: If you have Temporary Hit Points and take damage, those points are lost first, and any leftover damage carries over to your Hit Points. For example, if you have 5 Temporary Hit Points and take 7 damage, you lose those points and then lose 2 Hit Points
temporary_hit_points.duration: Temporary Hit Points last until they're depleted or you finish a Long Rest
temporary_hit_points.no_stacking: Temporary Hit Points can't be added together. If you have Temporary Hit Points and receive more of them, you decide whether to keep the ones you have or to gain the new ones. For example, if a spell grants you 12 Temporary Hit Points when you already have 10, you can have 12 or 10, not 22
temporary_hit_points.not_healing: Temporary Hit Points can't be added to your Hit Points, healing can't restore them, and receiving Temporary Hit Points doesn't count as healing. Because Temporary Hit Points aren't Hit Points, a creature can be at full Hit Points and receive Temporary Hit Points
temporary_hit_points.zero_hit_points: If you have 0 Hit Points, receiving Temporary Hit Points doesn't restore you to consciousness. Only true healing can save you

character_death.revival: If your character dies, others might find a magical way to revive your character, such as with the Raise Dead spell
character_death.new_character: Or talk with the DM about making a new character to join the group
character_death.reference: The Rules Glossary has more information on being dead
